On the contrary, the younger sons of nobles invariably tended to sink below the status of their fathers. Only some success in the process of social selection (favoured of course by origin) could help them to a social position that matched that of their eldest brother. Strategic opportunities for daughters were restricted to the choice of marriage candidates. 
Although the importance of wealth and habitus was at least as great in the reproduction of the English nobility as that of inherited privilege directly tied to the person, it is analytically advisable to consider the entire English nobility as a single estate. It is true that apart from the family heads and eldest sons of the peerage (a total that rarely reached more than a hundred), the English nobles individually possessed little privilege over and above their appropria-tion power as landlords - and in estate terms this was gradually neutralised. Collectively, however, they held considerable parts of the generalised power in their possession.”翻译为中文

除了地方贵族家庭从教区到郡的缓慢晋升外，还有向郡级贵族横向移动的途径。这向所有皇冠下的官员开放。这些职位确实通常保留给那些已被视为属于郡级家庭的成熟家族，但例如在都铎王朝，从不属于高级贵族或贵族低阶的家族中创建了强大的新贵族王朝。

形成包括高级贵族和贵族在内的习惯，源于三个结构性前提条件，其一是财产等级和地位等级（包括贵族内部的等级）的高度一致。

在十七世纪的法国，法官贵族的家庭可能比老贵族更富有，但在等级上远远低于他们，而在英格兰，来自社会可接受来源的巨大财富在决定晋升候选人——通过骑士爵位、准男爵头衔，最终可能晋升为贵族——中扮演了重要角色。

世袭头衔当然只能由皇冠授予，但由于高级贵族家族的排斥做法（那些排队等待贵族头衔的家族）没有得到政治制裁，对中央权力的范围和形式的冲突几乎未触及统治阶层的再生产。然而，这意味着进入乡绅阶层原则上对每个地主绅士都是开放的，只要他“和他的家”满足对高级和最高贵族制定的财富和习惯要求。

高级贵族和贵族文化和社会融合的另一个前提条件是贵族的政治权力条件。在英格兰，政治斗争也采取了宫廷阴谋的形式。然而，持久的政治影响力已经预设了在一个或多个郡的基础上（更多关于这一点见下文）。他们的统治实践和生活方式并没有使英格兰的贵族成为字面意义上的宫廷贵族，这一点自路易十四统治以来就是法国高级贵族存在的特点。

最后，贵族的习惯形成预设了高级贵族和贵族的占有实践之间差异的消失。由于即使是对高级贵族来说，通过武力占有的重要性也已经消失，贵族与高级贵族之间的利益差异相应缩小。这使得形成共同的贵族理想成为可能，从而导致荣誉观念的文化转变。

军官在卫队团的任职地位高于贵族的年轻子弟可选择的其他赚钱方式，以及在荣誉受损时的决斗社会义务，涉及到以个体战士的观念为导向的荣誉观。但是，传统的荣誉观念使得个体骑士成为决定性实例，甚至建立了对国王的抵抗权，而英格兰贵族的转变后荣誉观更多地面向为皇冠服务。

荣誉观念的适应改变了贵族的物质和社会再生产条件，也促进了贵族对宗教改革的文化处理。如果统治的道德化最初是由贵族成员推动的，那么这一观念现在被纳入了贵族的共同理想中。这也与封建时代和旧制度早期阶段对贵族家庭的“好客”理想提出的要求的解散有关。

“家庭”概念主要意味着对附庸的义务，后来对私人合同下的仆人的义务，“好客”特别是在十六世纪晚期和十七世纪早期成为贵族的规范，扩展到所有人，尤其是社区的穷人。这种好客也是宣称算作郡级绅士身份的决定性因素。因为在十六世纪甚至十七世纪，好客提供了获得社会声望的最重要方式之一。在高级贵族的清教徒家庭中，这也是认真基督教生活实践的一个组成部分。

这种行为理想不仅通过现在组织占有的方式成为可能。由于统治阶层的家庭经常将他们的土地租给大农场主，后者作为剥削制度的直接代表面对农民，而所有者如果偶尔阻止拖欠付款的小农民被驱逐，就可以被视为慷慨的，尤其是在这种行为对他们有用时，这可以归因于缺乏发达的地方警力的情况下发展家长式统治做法的必要性；同时，这种行为准则的宣传和部分实际实现，必须被认真看作是作为对该阶层自我认识的集体定义。在这个意义上，好客还用于展示土地有效统治者的身份。如果购买新的土地财产并未导致好客的实践，这在某种程度上表明了放弃融入当地统治圈子的意图。

随着好客特有的习性的发展，统治阶层成员的某种教育也随之发展。十六世纪和十七世纪的许多长子曾经上过大学或法学院之一。女儿们也通常接受基础教育。年轻的儿子们经常接受能让他们获得职位或法律实践的教育。

习性，即特定的视角和行为模式被精确地文化定义，成为进入贵族等级高位的最重要标准，至少在财产规模之后。除了贵族的长子外，没有政治制裁保证贵族成员会保持他们的社会地位。相反，贵族的年轻子弟总是倾向于降低他们父亲的地位。只有在社会选择过程中的一些成功（当然受到出身的支持）才能帮助他们获得与长兄相匹配的社会地位。女儿的战略机会则局限于选择婚姻对象。

尽管财富和习性的重要性在英格兰贵族的再生产中至少与直接与个人绑定的世袭特权一样大，但分析上建议将整个英格兰贵族视为一个单一阶层。的确，除了贵族家族的家长和长子（总数很少超过一百人）外，英格兰的贵族个体几乎没有比他们作为地主的占有权力更多的特权——并且从阶层角度来看，这逐渐被中和。然而，他们作为一个集体，仍拥有相当多的通用权力。

At the county level, however, the estate criteria of selection continued undiminished. In this way, members of the squirearchy secured favourable conditions for their practices of appropriation and their power to define the structures of good order.
'Good order'
Only when changes in the world destroyed those notions of order in which a renovator was condemned as a heretic, a man in rebellion against the will of God, did change in the content of good order come irrevocably into debate. Of the contemporary conceptions of God and the world, a good life and ‘good rule', the only aspect that is relevant here is that bearing on conflicts over the maintenance of old notions of order and the introduction of new ones. In this connection - as always in times of far-reaching change - the groups upholding competing notions of order were not identical with the ‘authorities' on the one hand and the governed (or lower orders) on the other. Even among those who actually or potentially participated in the practice of rule, there were diverging conceptions of what made for good order and with what methods this could best be maintained or established.
Before we turn to these differences, the first thing to note is that there were some definitions of good order that very many men and women accepted as 
unchallengeable in the sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries. We can con-clude from traditional models of behaviour and formulations in the context of protests, that this prevailing opinion was often shared even by those who according to Thomas Smith were ‘only embraced in the common wealth in so far as they are to be ruled'152 - in other words, day-labourers, poor peasants, small traders, artisans, women and children. We have good reason to suppose that the great majority of these shared the view that everyone should honour God and be subject to the king or queen, likewise servants and apprentices to their masters, children to their parents and women to their menfolk. In the event of a woman inheriting the kingdom, a duchy or earldom, the prevailing opinion made exceptions, because in this case the decisive thing was not age or sex, but the ‘blood' that had brought them into the world.153
Good order was conceived as a hierarchy, and its maintenance was seen as possible only if every person had a master. To describe someone as ‘master-less' under the ancien régime was to ascribe to them every kind of ill-discipline. This commandment of subjection to a master embraced everyone, even the most powerful nobles.
With the growth in population and the changed economic and social forms of profitable exploitation of landed property (not universal, but decisive in many regions), ‘masterlessness' became a constant problem for those authori-ties who had the task of establishing order and the means to achieve it. Views differed as to the methods that were required for this. And here we are already in the midst of the differing conceptions of the content and strategy of ‘good order'.
David Underdown has summed up the main tendencies of public-order policy in the following way: on the one hand, an orientation to tradition and custom, to forms of collaboration and the notion of a ‘harmonic' vertical soci-ety, and on the other hand, the attempt to achieve a moral reform(ation), to promote individualism and personal responsibility.154 As far as public-order strategies are concerned, the former tendency was directed towards achieving social stability by maintaining a quite definite structure anchored in custom. Public-order policy should therefore be directed to preventing change as far as possible. The other strategic position was oriented to dealing with the social consequences of change. Stability was understood here in a more 'formal' sense, i.e. as securing rule, but not particular cultural and social forms.
Simple schemata that ascribe different conceptions of order to representative social groups or institutions of rule are unsuccessful. For there were cultural conflicts in which estate differences and class antagonisms found expression, as well as conflicts between the more Puritan and more traditionally oriented members of the ruling estates. These did indeed partly coincide with the con-flict (to be explained below) between 'court' and 'country', but they were also overlaid by struggles over the actual extent of centralised authority. Finally, there were also significant regional differences. For depending on the form of production and settlement, the lower orders held more or less persistently to old conceptions of order, while their structures of communication gave them greater or lesser ability to defend these.155
To speak of differences between conceptions and strategies of public-order policy only makes analytical sense if the generalisation of rule has reached a certain point, so that generalised rule is no longer confined to fiscal appropria-tion and the prevention of internal war. This was the case in England at least from the second half of the sixteenth century.
In connection with the strategies for politically implementing the Reforma-tion and improving the organisation of armed force, monarchical rule became both more extensive and more intensive. It was not that the territorial lords of the time were dispossessed (the abolition of the previously autonomous administration in the episcopal seats of Ely, Durham and Hexham in 1536 is rather untypical of the general developmental trend), but some 'gaps' in the former practice of rule were filled in.
One of these gaps was spatial. For up to the Reformation, the validity of secular law stopped at religious 'sanctuaries'. Any man or woman of whatever station could escape pursuit by the authorities by flight to a sanctuary. The abolition of sanctuary meant the political sanctioning of practices that would previously have been seen as a monstrous sin. (As in many cases, it is necessary with the abolition of sanctuary to distinguish between the revo-lutionising of the legal situation and the persistence of modes of behaviour, which to some extent remain down to the present day).
A further 'gap' in generalised rule, and a far more significant one, was increasingly abolished in the course of the sixteenth century and the first decades of the seventeenth. In so far as the retinues of nobles grew rarer and smaller, there gradually ended the private organisation of armed force and, in this connection, also the practice of nobles to put pressure on courts by a show of force (on both of these, see above).
The constriction of the legal and practical room for manoeuvre of noble rule also had visible consequences. In the south of England at least, the lords increasingly abandoned the fortification of their dwellings.156
Following the far-reaching integration of the rule of the high nobility into the practice of generalised monarchical rule, two particular 'gaps' in this still remained. Manorial courts endured as an institution for the enforcement of private authority. Even if this rule was restricted by law, and - what was more important - by the resistance of the tenants, it still remained outside of the generalised rule. A further gap in generalised power was the so-called 'benefit of clergy'.

This privilege had been introduced in the twelfth century. It guaranteed that the majority of offences committed by clerics - exceptions were laid down - would be tried by church courts. Over the centuries, this clerical privilege was gradually extended to the better-off laity. Because literacy had at one time been largely confined to members of religious orders, a legal construction developed by which proof of literacy was equated with membership of a religious order. This clerical privilege was not open to appeal. In the sixteenth century, however, it was granted to many first offenders, who in practice were as good as acquitted. No one could plead benefit of clergy a second time. Courts oper-ated with the legal fiction that a first condemnation brought with it exclusion from the religious order, so that the person concerned no longer belonged to an order if they appeared before the court again. In the seventeenth century, the list of those offences for which clerical privilege could not be adduced in any case was widened. Benefit of clergy was finally abolished - and thus an estate-type gap in generalised royal judicial power filled - in 1827.157
A particular structural feature of the ancien régime in England was that the institution that developed into the most important instrument of monarchical power in the course of the sixteenth century represented at the same time a potential gap in this power. It was certainly a prerogative of the crown to appoint peace commissioners, remove Justices of the Peace and control the practice of these in office. Politically, however, the possibilities of exercising these powers were very limited, if a proposed or a sitting Justice was supported by locally influential families.
We shall discuss later on the regularisation of the practice of appointing ‘keepers of the peace' and later Justices of the Peace. What had originally been established as local instances of public order with limited tasks, developed under the Tudors into an institution that was to form the centre of local administrative and judicial power for a full three hundred years. Thomas Garden Barnes is of the view that this expansion of tasks and authority was so decisive that it can even be said that Justices of the Peace as well as lords lieutenant were an invention of the Tudor era.158
The crown's intention was to make the Justices of the Peace an executive organ of royal policy. The king's advisers, assembled regularly in Council, took care that legal provisions regarding the regulation of labour conditions, the disciplining of and aid to the poor and the punishment of vagabonds, were all made generally known. Increasing attention was paid to the instruction of travelling judicial commissions, with frequent instructions to the Justices of the Peace in the counties to check up on the local constables. Repeatedly from 1576, in the ‘books of orders', and especially in 1630-1, administrative instructions were given for dealing with inflation and plague. Since the crown needed to win support in the counties from men who had independent influence there, the implementation of its policy remained dependent on the king's aims being shared by locally dominant families. If this was not the case, then ‘negligence' arose, if not a regular opposing policy or in the worst case - as in the years after 1634 - refusal to serve as Justice of the Peace. On the whole, the effectiveness of local government practice improved the possibilities of implementing estate interests. If these coincided with the intentions of the crown, then Justices of the Peace were instruments of royal policy. If they ran against the goals of royal policy, then the power of the Justices of the Peace became an obstacle to the king's government.”翻译为中文

教区级别的职权特性，从某种程度上说变得更加有限，因为排斥的做法越来越针对整个贫困人口，而不再针对富裕的农民和大农场主。（这一发展从十七世纪下半叶开始加剧，尽管存在一定的地区差异。）取代了对地方普遍权力实践的专属阶层参与，教区级别出现了一种由财产和阶级利益主导的参与结构。然而，在郡一级，选择的阶层标准继续不减。通过这种方式，乡绅确保了他们占有实践和定义良好秩序结构的有利条件。

“良好秩序”
只有当世界的变化破坏了那些将改革者谴责为异端、反抗上帝意愿者的秩序观念时，良好秩序的内容变化才不可避免地成为争论的焦点。在当代对上帝和世界的观念、美好生活和“良好统治”的理念中，与维护旧秩序观念和引入新秩序观念的冲突相关的唯一方面才是此处相关的。在这方面——就像所有深刻变革时期一样——维护竞争秩序观念的群体并不与一方的“权威”和另一方的被统治者（或下层阶级）相同。即使在实际参与或有潜力参与统治实践的人中，也存在对何为良好秩序以及最佳维护或建立该秩序的方法的不同观念。

在讨论这些差异之前，首先要注意的是，在十六世纪和十七世纪早期，很多人接受了一些定义良好秩序的观念，认为这是不容置疑的。我们可以从传统行为模式和抗议背景中的表述中得出结论，这种普遍观点甚至常常为那些根据托马斯·史密斯的说法“只有在他们被统治的范围内才被纳入公共财富”的人所共享——换句话说，日工、贫穷的农民、小商人、手工艺者、女性和儿童。我们有充分的理由认为，这些人的绝大多数持有的观点是，每个人都应该尊敬上帝并服从国王或女王，同样，仆人和学徒要服从他们的主人，孩子服从他们的父母，女人服从他们的男性。在女性继承王国、公国或伯国的情况下，普遍观点做出了例外，因为在这种情况下，决定性的因素不是年龄或性别，而是将她们带入世界的“血统”。

良好秩序被构想为一种等级制度，只有每个人都有一个主人，其维护才被视为可能。在旧制度下将某人描述为“无主人”的，就是将他们归咎于各种不守纪律的行为。对一个主人的顺从戒律包括了每个人，甚至是最有权力的贵族。

随着人口增长和经济与社会形态的变化，使得土地财产的盈利性开发（虽然不是普遍的，但在许多地区是决定性的），“无主人状态”成为那些负责建立秩序及其手段的当局的一个持续问题。关于解决这一问题所需方法的观点存在分歧。而我们已经处于“良好秩序”的内容和策略的不同观念中了。

大卫·昂德伍德将公共秩序政策的主要倾向总结如下：一方面，是对传统和习俗的、合作形式的以及对“和谐”垂直社会观念的倾向，另一方面，是试图实现道德改革、促进个人主义和个人责任的尝试。就公共秩序策略而言，前一倾向旨在通过维护一种确切的、在习俗中根深蒂固的结构来实现社会稳定。因此，公共秩序政策应该尽可能地防止变化。另一战略立场则面向处理变化的社会后果。这里的稳定被理解为一种更“形式化”的意义，即作为确保统治，但不是特定文化和社会形式。

将不同的秩序观念归因于代表性的社会群体或统治机构的简单模式是不成功的。因为存在文化冲突，其中表达了阶层差异和阶级对立，以及统治阶层中更倾向清教徒和更倾向传统的成员之间的冲突。这些确实部分与“朝廷”和“乡村”之间的冲突（将在下文解释）重合，但也被关于中央权力实际范围的斗争所掩盖。最后，还存在重要的地区差异。因为根据生产和定居形式的不同，下层阶级对旧秩序观念的坚持程度不一，而他们的沟通结构赋予了他们更大或较小的能力来捍卫这些观念。

只有当统治的普遍化达到一定程度，以至于普遍化的统治不再仅限于财政占有和内战的预防时，讨论公共秩序政策的观念和策略差异才在分析上有意义。至少从十六世纪下半叶开始，英格兰就是这种情况。

与推进宗教改革的政治策略和改善武装力量的组织相关的策略，使得君主统治变得更加广泛和深入。并不是当时的领土领主被剥夺财产（1536年在埃利、达勒姆和海克瑟姆的主教治下的之前自治行政的废除，在总体发展趋势中相当不典型），而是一些以前统治实践中的“空白”被填补了。

其中一个“空白”是空间上的。直到宗教改革，世俗法律的有效性在宗教“庇护所”处停止。任何身份的男人或女人都可以通过逃亡到庇护所来逃避当局的追捕。庇护所的废除意味着政治上对此前被视为严重罪恶的做法的认可。（就像许多情况一样，在庇护所废除方面，需要区分法律形势的革命性变化和在一定程度上一直保留至今的行为模式。）

一个更为重要的普遍统治中的“空白”在十六世纪及十七世纪前几十年的过程中日益被废除。随着贵族的随从变得越来越稀少和缩小，私人武装力量的组织逐渐结束，与此相关，贵族通过武力展示向法庭施压的做法也随之结束。

贵族统治的法律和实践操作空间的收缩也有了明显的后果。至少在英格兰南部，领主们日益放弃加固他们的住所。

随着高级贵族的统治实践被广泛整合进君主普遍统治实践之后，这种普遍权力中仍然存在两个特殊的“空白”。领地法庭作为实施私人权威的机构继续存在。即使这种统治受到法律的限制，并且更重要的是受到租户的抵抗，但它仍然位于普遍统治之外。普遍权力的另一个“空白”是所谓的“教士豁免权”。

这种特权在十二世纪引入。它保证了大多数由教士犯下的罪行——除了规定的例外——将由教会法庭审理。几个世纪以来，这种教士特权逐渐扩展到较富裕的俗人中。因为曾经的读写能力大多限于宗教团体成员，因此发展出一种法律构造，将读写能力视同为宗教团体成员。教士豁免权不可上诉。然而，在十六世纪，它被授予许多初犯者，实际上等同于无罪释放。任何人都不能第二次请求教士豁免权。法庭运作的法律虚构是首次定罪带来了被排除出宗教团体，因此如果再次出现在法庭前，该人不再属于任何团体。在十七世纪，无法以任何情况下提出教士豁免权的罪行名单被扩大。教士豁免权最终在1827年被废除——从而填补了普遍皇家司法权力中的一个阶层性“空白”。

英格兰旧制度的一个特殊结构特征是，在十六世纪形成的、成为君主权力最重要工具的机构同时代表了这种权力中的潜在“空白”。的确，任命警察和撤销治安法官以及控制他们的职务实践是皇冠的特权。然而，政治上，如果被提名的或现任的治安法官得到了当地有影响力的家族的支持，行使这些权力的可能性非常有限。

稍后我们将讨论“维持和平者”任命实践的规范化及其后来的治安法官。最初作为具有限定任务的地方公共秩序实体而建立的，发展为都铎王朝下形成地方行政和司法权力中心三百年的机构。托马斯·加登·巴恩斯认为，任务和权威的扩大如此决定性，甚至可以说治安法官和郡中尉是都铎时代的发明。

皇冠的意图是使治安法官成为皇家政策的执行机构。国王的顾问们定期在议会中聚集，确保有关劳动条件的法律规定、对穷人的纪律和援助以及对流浪汉的惩罚都被普遍知晓。越来越多地注意指导巡回审判委员会，频繁指示郡的治安法官检查当地警长。从1576年开始，特别是在1630-31年的“命令书”中，反复就应对通货膨胀和瘟疫发布了行政指示。由于皇冠需要赢得郡中有独立影响力的人的支持，其政策的实施仍然依赖于国王的目标被当地占主导地位的家族所共享。如果不是这种情况，那么就会出现“疏忽”，如果不是正式的反对政策，那么在最糟糕的情况下 - 就像在1634年之后的几年里 - 拒绝担任治安法官。总的来说，地方政府实践的效力提高了实现阶层利益的可能性。如果这些与皇冠的意图一致，那么治安法官就是皇家政策的工具。如果它们与皇家政策的目标相悖，那么治安法官的权力就成了国王政府的障碍。

请将“The effective execution of generalised monarchical power in the counties required that ‘good order' be established by the application of laws and royal decrees. Yet obedience to the law, as many contemporaries affirmed, was not one of the outstanding qualities of the English population. Strategies aiming to enforce such obedience were seen by the groups involved, as they later were by historians, as an indispensable precondition for the establishment of orderly conditions. Only quite recently have scholars pointed out that, under the ancien régime too, there were procedures of maintaining orderly conditions that stood outside and in partial contradiction to the letter of the law, but served their purpose none the less. Strategies that aimed to make these procedures impossible, and instead tied the notion of public order precisely to royal law, amounted to a fundamental cultural transformation - in the truest sense of the term. This was the result of prolonged struggles, in the course of which one side had the advantage that it possessed the means of generalised power.
The public-order practice that was eventually established was directed either to maintaining peaceful coexistence in the context of the locally estab-lished forms of rule of the time, or to re-impose such peace if it had been dis-turbed. In this perspective, the strict execution of a legal provision (especially if this ran against long-practised modes of behaviour) could be seen precisely as a reason for discontent, as could the intervention of judicial process in a matter that could well have been dealt with outside the courts. It could sometimes promote coexistence in a community much more effectively if a victim of theft was satisfied with the return of the item stolen, than if he took his neighbour to court and brought misfortune to a whole family. (The notion of ‘neighbour' under the ancien régime embraced the entire immediate social milieu, rather then the stricter geographical meaning it has today.)
Even local constables often had recourse to traditional measures, whether because they shared the views of their neighbours or simply because they wanted to be tolerated by them. This meant that they too did not judge all thefts alike, but might react either with a warning or with an attempt to reach an appropriate settlement outside of court.159 And whereas brewing ale for the purpose of drinking bouts was certainly treated as an offence, they could 
only very reluctantly be led to report a villager who occasionally brewed his own liquor.160
If, however, things did come to a regular court case - often on the basis of private informants161 -, jurors were supposed to 'present' the case to the Justices whenever life or limb was at stake, Since such 'presentment juries' were responsibly for establishing the facts of the case, they had a decisive influence on the verdict. If the accused was a local person, then - in a formulation of J.H. Baker - his neighbours in their capacity as jurors formed a shield between him and the law.162 The criteria employed by these 'neighbours' were not neces-sarily more tolerant or just, but they were often different from those that the crown demanded.163
By strategies of both the crown and the Justices, the application of tradi-tional practices of public order was pressed back in the course of the sixteenth century: the authority of Justices in summary proceedings was expanded, so that formal court processes became more rare. This reduced the influence of jurors on judicial practice. When the collaboration of 'presentment juries' was prescribed, the exercise of traditional conceptions of public order could also be limited by judicial commissions demanding 'presentments' that were drafted with respect to legal provisions.164 Besides, until 1670, penalties could also be imposed on jurors who did not deliver their verdict according to the letter of the law.165 Justices of the Peace now took increasing care that local constables also acquired a certain knowledge of the law, even if they could not actually read. If they neglected this duty, they could count on being punished.
Whilst the expansion of the authority of Justices of the Peace affected the entire kingdom, there were wide regional, local and even individual differ-ences in the degree to which their practice ran counter to traditional public-order practices or the neglect of these. There were more than a few magistrates, moreover, who stuck to a more traditional notion of order. In this case, the exclusion of 'presentment juries' from the majority of cases, though it ruled 
out the influence of immediate neighbours, did not necessarily mean that the traditional idea of ‘good order' had been abandoned.
Correspondingly, Justices of the Peace only found it urgently necessary to set aside the independence of local constables and transform these into an instrument of authority, when they themselves sought to carry through a new public-order policy.
Most Justices of the Peace were concerned to carry out their office in such a way as not to damage the crown's goodwill. The change in the practice of pub-lic order was introduced - with some exceptions - above all by Justices who upheld puritanical (in the broadest sense) notions of ‘good order'. These used the opportunities with which the crown had provided them by the expansion of their authority, and which they had acquired by the disciplining of auxiliary forces, in order to achieve the moral reform of society. Their pressing goal was not the maintenance of a practice of rule that had proved itself by not provoking discontent, but rather the realisation of specific principles.
Many of these judicial ‘entrepreneurs of morality' (K. Wrightson) vigorously campaigned against traditional festivals and Sunday entertainments in the early decades of the seventeenth century. They sought to eradicate drunk-enness, whoring and laziness root and branch, and took pains to ensure that Sunday was kept sacred and family ties respected. Many of these moral rig- orists were prepared if need be to confront not only the lower orders, but also their equals and the king's advisers.
Conflict with the crown arose in particular over the attitude towards Sun-day entertainments. Whilst many Justices would allow no sport apart from archery (useful for military purposes), and even this only after the end of a very long Sunday service, the crown passed laws in 1617, 1618 and especially 1633 that permitted a whole range of Sunday entertainments. Those Justices who sought to intervene on Sundays to prevent sin were told by their opponents that football was ‘in the book'. Embittered Justices saw the ‘book of sports' as evidence of the corrupt morality of the court. David Underdown points out that these laws represented, for the first time in England, a manipulative attitude to the stabilising contents of traditional popular culture.166
Forms of public communication such as those offered by market and feast days, and by sporting competitions, had always contained the possibility of 
erupting into rebellious behaviour, but at the same time they signified fixed points in the annual course of a public life that was integrated into the practice of traditional rule. Only when the moral reformers forced members of the lower orders to defend the manner and form in which they sought recreation, did dancing, card playing and enthusiasm for a good game of football come to be seen as acts of resistance against authority. Whether such resistance was actually practised, to what extent and with what success, depended not least - as Underdown has shown - on the regionally dominant forms of agricultural production and the patterns of settlement and communication that corre-sponded to these.167
Defence of custom as the guiding thread of public-order practice remained, on the whole, confined to particular spheres. If Justices went so far as to whip not only young 'vagabonds' and put them in the pillory, but old 'vagabonds' as well,168 this was a departure from custom. The same went for the constables if they reported 'vagabonds' or even arrested them themselves. J.S. Sharpe reports how in 1621 villagers attempted to prevent the imprisonment of a 'vagabond'. There must have been many instances of this kind, whether documented or not, but they were not typical. The case rather seems to have been that in the course of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries wandering beggars lost whatever protection they had traditionally enjoyed - limited though this may have been - on the basis of the commandment of Christian charity and noble hospitality.
On the whole, the criminal practices that made up the actual core of Eliza-bethan Poor Law met with little resistance.169 For example, in a district of Lan-cashire that has been studied in detail, it was reported that the local constables increasingly reported 'vagabonds' in the 1630, though offences by local per-sons continued to be reported only rarely.170 The harshness of the law was thus applied selectively according to social criteria, and in this the forces of order enjoyed - on the whole - the support of the population. (At the general level of our discussion here, the differences between village and towns - with the exception of London - were no greater than those between different rural 
districts.) This attitude can be explained principally because the number of itinerant poor had grown since the second half of the sixteenth century, while after the wars of the late sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries, many dis-charged soldiers (and in 1627-8 many deserters) wandered the countryside and resorted to begging until they could find new work. At least equally deci-sive, however, were the changed preconditions for the social perception of poverty, since public-order practices were determined not by the objective situation, but by the shape and form in which this situation was regarded and defined as a 'problem' in (power-structured) public discourse (according to P. Slack).171 It was in this connection that the differences between the material conditions of life of members of the non-privileged orders, which had grown since the second half of the sixteenth century, acquired their significance for public-order policy.
With the formation of a group of materially better-off families who regarded themselves as righteous, and the simultaneously deteriorating situation of members of the lowest social strata, tendencies to socially exclude the poor were increasingly marked. Initially the poor from other districts were discrim-inated against, then also the local poor. In these processes of social differen-tiation (if not outright polarisation), Poor Law repression obtained a support that went far beyond the ruling orders. As a result of these social changes, the preconditions for traditional ways of securing peace in the neighbourhood also disappeared - at different rates according to the particular region.”翻译为中文

在郡一级，有效执行普遍化的君主权力要求通过应用法律和国王法令建立“良好秩序”。然而，正如许多同时代人所确认的，对法律的服从并不是英格兰人民的突出品质之一。旨在强制执行此类服从的策略被涉及群体视为建立有序条件不可或缺的前提条件，后来历史学家也持同样看法。只是最近学者们才指出，在旧制度下，也存在维护有序条件的程序，这些程序在一定程度上与法律的字面含义相矛盾，但仍然达到了其目的。旨在使这些程序不可能的策略，而是将公共秩序的概念准确地与皇家法律联系起来，相当于一次根本性的文化转变——在最真实的意义上。这是长期斗争的结果，其中一方因拥有普遍权力的手段而占据优势。
最终建立的公共秩序实践旨在维护当时地方建立的统治形式下的和平共处，或在和平受到干扰时重新强加和平。从这个角度来看，严格执行法律规定（特别是如果这与长期实践的行为模式相悖）可能正是不满的原因，司法程序干预本可以在法庭外解决的事务也是如此。如果盗窃受害者对偷窃物品的归还感到满意，有时可能比将邻居告上法庭并给整个家庭带来不幸更能有效促进社区内的共处。（在旧制度下，“邻居”的概念包括整个直接的社会环境，而不是它今天更严格的地理含义。）
即使当地警官也经常采取传统措施，无论是因为他们与邻居持相同观点，还是仅仅因为他们希望被邻居容忍。这意味着他们也不会将所有盗窃行为一概而论，而可能会做出警告，或尝试在法庭外达成适当和解。而且，尽管为聚会酿造麦芽酒肯定被视为一种违法行为，但他们很不情愿报告偶尔酿造自酿酒的村民。
但是，如果事情确实发展到了正式的法庭案件——经常基于私人线人——陪审员应该在生命或肢体受到威胁时将案件"陈述"给法官。由于这些“陈述陪审团”负责查明案件事实，他们对判决有决定性影响。如果被告是当地人，那么就如J.H.贝克尔的说法，他的邻居作为陪审员在他和法律之间形成了一道屏障。这些“邻居”采用的标准不一定更宽容或公正，但往往与皇室要求的不同。
皇室和法官的策略使传统的公共秩序实践在十六世纪的过程中受到了压制：治安法官在简易程序中的权威得到了扩大，从而使正式的法庭程序变得更加罕见。这减少了陪审员对司法实践的影响。当要求“陈述陪审团”的协作时，也可以通过司法委员会要求的根据法律规定起草的“陈述”来限制传统的公共秩序观念的行使。此外，直到1670年，如果陪审员没有根据法律的字面意义作出裁决，还可以对他们处以处罚。治安法官现在越来越注意确保地方警官也获得了一定的法律知识，即使他们实际上可能不识字。如果他们忽视这一职责，他们可以指望被惩罚。
虽然治安法官的权威扩展影响了整个王国，但在他们的实践与传统公共秩序实践或忽视这些实践的程度上存在广泛的地区、地方甚至个体差异。此外，还有不少法官坚持较传统的秩序观念。在这种情况下，将“陈述陪审团”排除在大多数案件之外，虽然排除了直接邻居的影响，但并不一定意味着传统的“良好秩序”观念已被抛弃。
相应地，只有当治安法官自己寻求实施新的公共秩序政策时，才迫切需要搁置地方警官的独立性并将其转变为权威工具。
大多数治安法官都希望以不损害皇室好感的方式执行他们的职责。公共秩序实践的变化——一些例外除外——主要是由那些坚持清教徒（广义上）“良好秩序”观念的法官引入的。这些人利用了皇冠通过扩大他们的权威提供的机会，以及他们通过对辅助力量进行纪律化所获得的机会，以实现社会的道德改革。他们的迫切目标不是维持一种通过不引起不满而证明自身的统治实践，而是实现特定原则。
许多这些司法“道德企业家”（K. 莱特森）在十七世纪早期积极反对传统节日和星期天娱乐活动。他们寻求根除酗酒、嫖娼和懒惰，努力确保星期天被视为神圣并且家庭关系受到尊重。许多这些道德严苛者准备在必要时不仅对抗下层阶级，而且对抗他们的同辈甚至国王的顾问。
与皇室的冲突特别是围绕对星期天娱乐活动的态度展开的。尽管许多法官只允许射箭（出于军事目的），甚至这也只能在非常长的星期天服务结束后进行，皇室在1617年、1618年和特别是1633年通过了允许一系列星期天娱乐活动的法律。那些试图在星期天干预以阻止罪行的法官被对手告知踢足球是“书中所说”。愤怒的法官将“体育活动书”视为法庭道德腐化的证据。大卫·昂德伍德指出，这些法律首次代表了英格兰对传统民间文化稳定内容的操纵态度。
市场和节日，以及体育竞赛等公共交流形式一直包含爆发为反抗行为的可能性，但同时也标志着传统统治实践中公共生活年度进程的固定点。只有当道德改革者强迫下层阶级为捍卫他们寻求娱乐的方式和形式而进行抗争时，跳舞、打牌和对一场好足球赛的热情才被视为对权威的抵抗行为。这种抵抗是否真正实践，以及在多大程度上和以何种成功取决于——正如昂德伍德所示——农业生产的区域主导形式以及与之相对应的定居和沟通模式。
整体而言，对习俗作为公共秩序实践的指导原则的捍卫主要局限于特定领域。如果法官不仅鞭打年轻的“流浪汉”并将他们置于颜面架上，而且对老“流浪汉”也这样做，那么这就偏离了习俗。同样，如果警官举报“流浪汉”甚至亲自逮捕他们。J.S.夏普报告说，在1621年，村民试图阻止一名“流浪汉”的监禁。这种情况一定有很多，无论是否有记录，但它们并不典型。相反，似乎在十六和十七世纪的过程中，流浪的乞丐失去了他们传统上所享有的保护——尽管这可能是有限的——基于基督教慈善和贵族好客的命令。
总的来说，伊丽莎白时代《贫困法》的实际核心构成的刑事实践遇到的抵抗很少。例如，在详细研究的兰开夏郡的一个地区，据报道，当地警官在1630年代日益举报“流浪汉”，尽管对当地人的违法行为继续很少报道。因此，法律的严厉性根据社会标准有选择性地应用，而在这方面，秩序力量在整体上得到了人们的支持。（在我们这里的讨论中，除伦敦外，村庄和城镇之间的差异并不大于不同农村地区之间的差异。）这种态度主要可以解释为，自十六世纪下半叶以来，流浪贫困者的数量增加，而在十六世纪末和十七世纪初的战争之后，许多退伍士兵（在1627-8年许多逃兵）在农村流浪并求助乞讨，直到他们能找到新工作。然而，至少同样决定性的是，贫困的社会感知前提条件的改变，因为公共秩序实践不是由客观情况决定的，而是由这种情况被如何看待和定义为“问题”的（权力结构化的）公共话语的形状和形式决定的（根据P. 斯莱克）。在这方面，自十六世纪下半叶以来非特权阶层成员的生活物质条件之间的差异增大，这对公共秩序政策具有重要意义。
随着认为自己为正直的、物质条件较好的家庭群体的形成，以及最低社会阶层成员状况的恶化，越来越多地倾向于社会排斥贫困者。最初是来自其他地区的穷人受到歧视，然后也包括本地穷人。在这些社会分化过程中（如果不是彻底的两极分化），《贫困法》的压迫获得了远远超出统治阶层的支持。由于这些社会变化，传统维护邻里和平的方式的前提条件也消失了——根据不同地区的不同速度。

请将“What appeared in their place - increasingly after the sixteenth century - was a public-order strategy that continued through to the nineteenth century to serve the ancien régime in England as a typical way of dealing with social unrest. Its application was provoked by the fact that even those inhabitants of villages and towns who did not seek to be seen as respectable and were constantly in need, had only two possibilities of making their claims known: petition or rebellious behaviour. The limits were fluid, as 'trouble-makers' generally also petitioned, and the authorities often defined even the process of petitioning as rebellion. If the demands made by way of petition and 'dis-turbance' were recognised at the local level as justified or at least understand-able, many Justices applied a procedure that was not envisaged in the laws of the kingdom or the decrees of the Privy Council, using the power of their office to settle social conflicts politically. They were all the more ready to do so if the protestors stuck to forms that affirmed their basic faithfulness to the law.
That 'trouble-makers' were very largely aware of these preconditions is shown by the formulations of their petitions, not least by the fact that in cases of inflammatory price rises bread was not simply appropriated, but sold at the price that was deemed as just and the proceeds paid out to the bakers. This perspective also explains the attempt by protestors that J. Walter reports, for legal advice in respect of their demands and behaviour.172
Local conditions, and the specific conduct of rebels and magistrates, varied, as did the occasions that provoked rebellion. The numerous studies of particular 'disturbances' that we now have to hand, however, demonstrate a fairly clear pattern of a regular procedure for settling conflicts that was set under way by both sides.
It was not rare for Justices to use the occasion of 'disturbances' to protest against particular measures of the crown - such as the granting of export licenses in times of inflation.173 Most of them were only prepared to enforce the drastic measures that the Privy Council prescribed for all cases of social unrest, if they feared for the property of the wealthy or even their own life and limb. If this was the case, then draconian punishment was applied - just as in other societies of the 'ancien-régime' structure. A differentiated response to demands of the (lower) people, however, must have been sufficiently common for the 'rebels' to submit time and again to the unwritten laws of this practice.
If the Justices of the Peace, either by themselves or (as was later the general rule) together with other members of the responsible Commission of the Peace, negotiated with rebels and on occasion sought to abolish particular conditions that had inflamed them, they were guided in the first place by local requirements for the maintenance or restoration of orderly conditions, and not - as the Privy Council demanded - by general legal provisions. This practical criticism of a universal validity of the equation of 'order' and 'law' 
continued the old practice of achieving local peace by 'neighbourly' compul-sion. But the continuity ended here. For the 'settlement' of the Justices was an exercise of rule: the precondition for it was that the criteria for the legitimating of modes of behaviour by 'neighbours' had disappeared in the course of the sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries. Besides, the Justices possessed not just the means of social discrimination, but also those of specific and draco-nian punishment. The institutionalisation of estate-based local government practice revolutionised the social character of public-order practice outside the courts.
b.3. 'The charms of the sovereign'
In Section b.1 we announced the project of tracing the particular structural characteristics of the ancien régime in England by an analytical distinction between centralised and generalised power - in other words, between the policy of the crown and the practice of royal government by members of the ruling estates. The potential difference between the political goals that were pursued in each of these two spheres of royal policy formed a structural pre-condition for crises in the system of rule. Before this thesis is developed in more detail, it is necessary to warn against possible misunderstandings.
The distinction between centralised and generalised power remains purely analytical, in so far as all means of generalised power belonged to the reigning monarch in person. The application of judicial power on the basis of general laws and principles of common law, the command of armed men in war and the collection of taxes (down to contributions for the poor that were raised at parish level), took place in the name of the king or queen. This generalised power, however, was practised by men (in rare cases, such as the administra-tion of wardships or forest rights, possibly also by women), who served the king or queen in this way. This personal character of rule is not easy for social scientists of today to comprehend. They are inclined therefore to see the ser-vants of reigning monarchs as executive organs of an early modern state, and to equate this generalised power with the 'public power' that is customary in modern times. Ernst Kantorowicz, for example, concluded from the contem-porary doctrine of the 'two bodies of the king' that royal rule in England under the ancien régime was distinct from the person of the king and conceived as a power of public office. As evidence of this conception, Kantorowicz adduced 
the quasi-legal argument that was made by Parliament when it took up arms against Charles I in 1642.174 Frederic William Maitland, on the other hand, saw this rhetoric of revolution as no evidence for structural developments having already taken place, no more did George E. Aylmer later on. Aylmer held to the fundamental structural difference between service to the king and a state bureaucracy, and argued that until 1649 there were no indications of the latter as yet in England.175 Maitland emphasised that the king's rule in England was not developed until the seventeenth century as a power of office distinct from the person of the king, rather the arbitrary power of the king that was in principle unlimited had to be removed step by step in the form of legal regu- lation.176 If kings kept to such limitations in their practice of government, they did not thereby become organs of a public power. The government remained the property of a royal person, but disposal over this property was limited by custom, by formalised ‘arrangements' and specific conditions of rule.
In the process of formation of the ancien régime, i.e. the generalisation of estate interests, it was especially the king's disposal of feudal tenures that was to a large extent withdrawn (by fiscalisation). Kings thus lost those founda-tions of their power that for the sake of simplicity we sum up as ‘feudal'. But this was far from amounting to the decisive weakening of personal rule, and thus the beginning of modern statehood, that has frequently been supposed. For out of the (above described) generalisation of estate interests into an instance that sanctioned private power of appropriation and whose authority also extended to the political mastery of social crises, personal monarchical rule obtained a new social basis.177 This development became possible and was strengthened by the ‘end of rebellions' of the high nobility, which Law-rence Stone has treated in detail.178 Since the nobles lost to a large extent their private possession of armed force, they became court nobles. (A term that refers here to the conditions of reproduction of their political power.)
The structural change in the basis of royal rule was expressed in the formula of the ‘King in Parliament'. This did not mean that Parliament had appropri-ated the previous royal possession of rule. Even after the Reformation this 
was not the case - as noted above. When Henry VIII set out to free the English kingdom from the sphere of papal rule, he used Parliament as an 'instrument' of his policy.179 This policy cost him lands, titles, and tenancy concessions, but it did not lead to kings and queens having to make future decisions of religious policy even in agreement with Parliament.
When, in 1604, James I decisively rejected the demands of the Puritans for the disempowerment of bishops, at the theological conference he had called at Hampton Court, this occurred in the context of the Privy Council and not in Parliament.180 This did not lessen in any way the effect of his decision, nor did it lead to any kind of crisis for the church or the monarch's own power.
The 'King in Parliament' continued to be the king in his Parliament, i.e. an assembly that was called and dismissed at the request of the monarch, and whose participants, though they might well permit themselves forceful criticism of the present course of government, would attend Parliament again after a spell of imprisonment in the Tower of London. For this reason, it is also proper to speak of 'parliaments' in the plural rather than in the singular. The establishment of Parliament as such, an institution independent of the will of the monarch, was a revolutionary act. This institutionalisation of Parliament was only decisively accomplished at the turn of the eighteenth century (cf. below).
Parliaments could be used for the settlement of disputes (in the form of 'private bills'). They served to inform the king or queen - in pressing terms, if necessary - as to the conditions of their rule. Only in relation to the granting of taxes, however, was it established under the ancien régime that reigning monarchs, if they wanted to effectively collect the taxes they had demanded, had to seek the approval of Parliament. The possibility of refusing such taxes, however, as we have already shown, was quite limited, and the extra-parlia-mentary practice of royal fiscal power in no way completely eliminated.
In the phase of the ancien régime under discussion here, the centre of policy did not lie in Parliament, not even in the connection of Parliament and the Privy Council. The centre of policy was rather the court. It was at court that factions were formed - on the basis of competition for patronage, personal intrigue, kinship connections and serious political convictions - and sought 
to influence the king or queen. Sometimes these factions lasted only a short while, occasionally they persisted for years. When Parliaments were called - as a temporary expansion of the Privy Council - these court factions sought to win broader influence there. In this connection, the influence that great nobles exercised on members of the Commons by the use of their patronage was of particular significance for the government of England.
The view upheld by many authors that the Commons was politically com-pletely in the hands of influential lords, overlooks the fact that members of the gentry, if they profited from patronage, did not necessarily have to see themselves as 'bought'. The lords, too, were required for their part to take into account local expectations, if they wanted to be powerful in one or more counties, and on this basis have influence at court.”翻译为中文

在十六世纪之后，取代之前的做法的是一种公共秩序策略，这种策略一直延续到十九世纪，成为英格兰旧制度处理社会动荡的典型方式。其应用是由于即使是那些不寻求被视为体面人士并且经常处于困境的村庄和城镇居民，也只有两种方式来表达他们的诉求：请愿或叛乱行为。界限是模糊的，因为被认为是“制造麻烦的人”通常也会请愿，当局经常把请愿过程本身定义为叛乱。如果地方层面承认通过请愿和“骚乱”提出的要求是正当的或至少是可以理解的，许多法官会应用一种不在王国法律或枢密院法令中预见的程序，利用他们的职权政治解决社会冲突。如果抗议者坚持表达他们对法律的基本忠诚的形式，法官们会更愿意这样做。

“制造麻烦的人”非常清楚这些前提条件，这一点从他们请愿书的措辞中可以看出，尤其是在价格上涨引发不满的情况下，面包不是简单地被占有，而是以被认为公正的价格出售，收入支付给面包师。这一视角也解释了抗议者寻求关于他们的要求和行为的法律建议的尝试。

地方条件、叛乱者和法官的具体行为以及引发叛乱的原因各不相同。然而，我们现在拥有的关于特定“骚乱”的众多研究展示了双方开启解决冲突的常规程序的相当清晰的模式。

法官使用“骚乱”的场合抗议皇室的特定措施（如在通货膨胀时期授予出口许可）并不少见。大多数法官只有在担心富人的财产甚至是他们自己的生命和财产安全时，才会执行枢密院为所有社会动荡情况规定的严厉措施。如果是这种情况，就会施加严厉的惩罚——就像在其他“旧制度”结构的社会中一样。然而，对（下层）民众的要求做出差异化反应在相当普遍程度上必须是常见的，因为“叛乱者”一次又一次地提交到这种实践的不成文法则之下。

如果治安法官，不论是独自一人还是（后来成为普遍规则）与其他负责的治安委员会成员一起，与叛乱者进行谈判，并在某些情况下寻求消除激发他们不满的特定情况，他们首先是被当地维护或恢复有序条件的需求所指导，而不是——如枢密院要求的那样——由一般法律规定所指导。对‘秩序’与‘法律’等同的普遍有效性的这种实践性批评，延续了通过‘邻里’压力实现地方和平的旧实践。但连续性到此为止。因为法官的“和解”是一种统治行为：其前提条件是‘邻里’通过合法化行为方式的标准在十六世纪和十七世纪初期的过程中已经消失。此外，法官不仅拥有社会歧视的手段，还拥有特定和严厉的惩罚手段。基于阶层的地方政府实践的制度化，革命性地改变了法庭外公共秩序实践的社会特性。

在第b.1节中，我们宣布了追溯英格兰旧制度的特定结构特征的计划，通过区分中央集权和普遍权力——换句话说，区分皇室政策和统治阶层成员的皇家政府实践。这两个领域的皇家政策所追求的政治目标之间潜在的差异形成了统治体系危机的结构性前提条件。在更详细地发展这一论点之前，有必要警告可能的误解。

中央集权与普遍权力之间的区分纯粹是分析性的，因为所有普遍权力的手段都属于在位君主个人。基于一般法律和普通法原则的司法权力的应用、在战争中指挥武装人员和征税（直至在教区级别征收的穷人捐款），都是以国王或女王的名义进行的。然而，这种普遍权力是由为国王或女王服务的男性（在少数情况下，如监护权或森林权的管理，可能也由女性）实践的。当代社会科学家很难理解这种统治的个人特性。因此，他们倾向于将在位君主的仆人视为早期现代国家的执行机关，并将这种普遍权力等同于现代习惯中的“公共权力”。例如，恩斯特·康托罗维茨从“国王的两体说”的当代理论得出结论，英格兰在旧制度下的王权不同于国王的个人，被视为公职权力。作为这一观念的证据，康托罗维茨援引了1642年议会对抗查理一世时提出的准法律论点。另一方面，弗雷德里克·威廉·梅特兰认为，这种革命的修辞并不是结构发展已经发生的证据，乔治·E·艾尔默后来也持有相同观点。艾尔默坚持认为为国王服务与国家官僚制之间有根本的结构区别，并认为直到1649年，在英格兰还没有迹象表明后者已经出现。梅特兰强调，国王在英格兰的统治直到十七世纪才作为一种与国王个人不同的公职权力发展起来，而国王原则上不受限制的专制权力必须通过法律逐步去除。如果国王在其统治实践中遵守这些限制，他们并没有因此成为公共权力的机关。政府仍然是一个王室个人的财产，但对这一财产的支配受到习俗、正式化的“安排”和特定统治条件的限制。
在旧制度的形成过程中，即阶层利益的普遍化过程中，国王对封建领地的支配在很大程度上被剥夺（通过财政化）。因此，国王失去了为简单起见我们总结为“封建”的那些权力基础。但这远未达到通常所假设的对个人统治的决定性削弱，从而开启现代国家的开始。因为从（上述描述的）阶层利益的普遍化成为一个制裁私人占有权力的实例和其权威也扩展到政治掌控社会危机的过程中，个人君主统治获得了新的社会基础。这种发展在高级贵族“叛乱”的终结中得以实现和加强，劳伦斯·斯通对此进行了详细论述。由于贵族在很大程度上失去了私人武装力量的拥有，他们成为了宫廷贵族。（这一术语在这里指的是他们政治权力再生产的条件。）
王权基础的结构性变化体现在“议会中的国王”这一说法中。这并不意味着议会已经占有了之前国王的统治权力。正如上文所述，即使在宗教改革之后也不是这种情况。当亨利八世着手将英格兰王国从教廷统治的范围中解放出来时，他将议会用作他政策的“工具”。这一政策让他失去了土地、头衔和租赁特许，但并没有导致国王和女王在未来的宗教政策决策上甚至需要与议会达成一致。
当詹姆士一世在1604年在他召集的汉普顿宫神学会议上坚决拒绝清教徒关于剥夺主教权力的要求时，这发生在枢密院的背景下而不是在议会中。这丝毫没有减少他的决定的影响，也没有引发任何教会或君主自己权力的危机。
“议会中的国王”仍然是他自己的议会，即一个应君主的要求而召集和解散的集会，尽管参与者可能会允许自己对当前的政府方针进行有力的批评，但在伦敦塔被监禁一段时间后，他们会再次出席议会。因此，也适合用复数而不是单数来谈论“议会”。作为独立于君主意志的机构确立议会本身，是一项革命行为。这种议会的制度化直到十八世纪末才得以决定性完成（见下文）。
议会可用于解决争议（以“私人法案”的形式）。它们用于通知国王或女王——如果必要，可以强烈地——他们统治的条件。然而，根据旧制度，在征税问题上确立了一个原则，即在位的君主如果想要有效征收他们要求的税款，必须寻求议会的批准。然而，拒绝这种税款的可能性是相当有限的，且国王在议会外的财政权力实践并未完全消除。
在这里讨论的旧制度阶段，政策的中心并不在议会，甚至不在议会和枢密院的联系中。政策的中心实际上是宫廷。正是在宫廷，基于对赞助的竞争、个人阴谋、亲属关系和严肃的政治信仰，形成了派系，并试图影响国王或女王。有时这些派系只持续很短时间，有时它们持续多年。当议会召开时——作为枢密院的临时扩展——这些宫廷派系试图在那里赢得更广泛的影响。在这方面，大贵族通过利用他们的赞助在下议院成员中行使的特殊影响，对英格兰的治理具有特别重要的意义。

许多作者坚持的观点，即下议院在政治上完全掌握在有影响力的贵族手中，忽视了一个事实，即如果贵族从赞助中获益，他们不一定必须视自己为“被收买的”。贵族们也需要考虑到地方期望，如果他们想在一个或多个郡拥有权力，并以此在宫廷中有影响力。

请将“The court, moreover, was not just the centre of policy, it was also a means of policy. By creating a splendid surrounding for the royal person, the court represented the personal structure of rule. In court ceremonial this form of rule was staged afresh each day - with distributed roles. The more that the art of representation developed, in the historical epoch that art historians formerly described as the Renaissance, and the more successfully this art began to spread over time to the various princely and royal courts of Europe, the more impressive was the new definition of personal rule that now materialised. This did not replace the foundation of personal rule in power and religion, it was rather the expression of an established structure of rule, generalised in 'institutional' forms. The magnificence of power not only became itself a power factor, but also a justification presupposed and inherent in it. At the various princely and royal courts, the new social forms of presentation of personal rule, and the external attributes corresponding to these, rapidly acquired quite different forms. We cannot deal in any detail here with this development. The following presentation will be confined to highlighting two circumstances that influenced the structural change in the definition of personal monarchical power in England in a specific fashion.
One of the most important political functions of the court lay in the power of conviction that the secular cult of the royal person could wield. For the cer-emonial surrounding the person of the king cast a quite special light on royal decrees. It was of particular significance, therefore, that the cult of the king's person developed in very particular historical circumstances in England. Its origins stretch back to the reign of Henry VIII, thus to a time when opposition 
to papal rule became integral to the definition of the personal rule of English kings. At first, this definition was not yet irrevocable. Only with the reign of Elizabeth did anti-papism, i.e. the struggle against views and policies that rightly or wrongly were seen as supporting papal rule, become a fixed com-ponent of the politically dominant discourse. This made the eagerness for cor-rect belief into a new (additional) basis for the personal power of the English monarchs - at the end of the sixteenth century. It can be described, with all due caution, as the formation of a plebiscitary element in personal royal rule.
The facts that Elizabeth was a woman and that the enemies of 'England' were Catholic were both favourable to this development. When Elizabeth's reign began, the crown had already lost Calais (its last possession on the Continent), the coffers were empty, the Reformation was on the retreat, her claim to the throne was challenged and she was surrounded by Catholic ene-mies. Whether there really did develop between Elizabeth and the English people that 'bond of love' lasting for forty-five years that Garrett Mattingly describes,181 or whether historians too have been taken in by the legend of Good Queen Bess, it remains incontestable that the actual weaknesses of the English crown, and the supposed weaknesses of the unmarried (and hence supposedly virgin) woman, were turned into a strength of her personal rule.
This development found expression in the 'Association' that was founded in 1585 for the protection of the royal person in case of an invasion. The for-mation of this Association harked back to forms of medieval vassalage and loyalty, thus to structures of obligation to which the English monarchs had for a long time ceased to lay claim. More significant, however, was the readiness to contribute to the organisation and formation of the militia. The inconve-niences that attended the muster could be borne more readily, not just by the prospect of beer, but also by the fact that it was seen as service to a lady whom many honoured already in her lifetime as the 'faery queen'.182
Elizabeth's lifetime also brought much experience of the harshness of royal caprice, and much rumour of unheard-of presents to the queen's favourites. Yet it was not just painters, poets, courtiers and clerics who idealised the queen. In the popular idealisation of Good Queen Bess - which continued to circulate after her death - a new element was added to the definition 
of personal royal rule: the idea that this rule was not just the property of a person, but could rather be deployed as a service to the good of all. Under the particular circumstances of female succession to the throne and the wars against 'papists', there rose in late sixteenth-century England the beginnings of the concept of rule that was later summed up elsewhere in the notion of the father or mother of the country. That neither James I nor Charles I could meet this new expectation of royal governing practice must be assumed to have marked discourse about their government in a specific way. (The interpretation offered here is that criticism of the governments of the two early Stuarts should not be understood exclusively as a reaction to the specific content and form that these took, but also referred to the way that in the reign of Elizabeth new ideas of personal rule arose that henceforth became effective requirements.)
The 'charms of the sovereign' that blossomed so particularly under Eliza-beth were cultivated at all royal courts of the ancien régime. They became a component of the social forms in which the 'policy of the crown' came about. The cult of the royal person facilitated the prosecution of this policy, while at the same time the presentation of royal splendour and magnificence concealed new elements of crisis within it. In different ways, these dangers became apparent in the reigns of the two Stuart kings.
The personal character of royal rule was embodied, among other things, by the fact that royal favour could procure money and land even irrespective of established positions of power and 'justified expectations', also that it was not completely calculable, but continued to depend on individual propensities and moods of the royal person. This arbitrariness of royal favour was a material precondition for the reproduction of the social forms of court life, which was organised around the 'proximity' of a person. It was at the same time - and for England in particular - a structural precondition that could generate crises of government. There were three particular reasons for this: because the court nobles of England were concerned to procure and maintain a political basis in the counties and hence also in the Commons by way of patronage; because the crown could not alienate its creditors, especially the privileged corporations of London; and because new (estate) privileges could damage the maintenance of traditional forms of appropriation and for securing rule.
The example of the soap monopoly, which at first sight might seem inciden-tal, already indicates certain difficulties inherent in the policy of privileges. 
David Underdown's study of the cultural norms and political orientations of the lower orders makes clear the potential scope of these. When Charles I granted courtiers permission to enclose wooded areas and turn them into pri-vate property, or to drain marshes, what was reduced in the first instance were the reproduction prospects of the poorer rural population. If these rebelled, however, they were often supported by the better-off, and even by the members of the local gentry. Modernisations of this kind, moreover, also had an effect on the appropriation opportunities of the more well-placed strata, and above all they reinforced opposition to a policy that favoured outsiders. By granting privileges to courtiers, the crown dealt a blow to traditional structures of reproduction and practice of rule, and proved itself a danger to peace and order in the counties.183 Social conflict thus developed into criticism of the government. Rebellions against enclosure did not express so much an antago-nism between classes, as rather a conflict between greedy upstarts on the one hand and defenders of local order on the other.
It was not just in relation to the political requirement and simultaneous political risk of granting privileges that the new emphasis on the personal character of royal power signified both a growth in this power but at the same time a new danger for it. For new possibilities of government crisis arose in so far as monarchs and their advisers could themselves be confused by the representation of royal power, and forget the fact that English kings were powerful only to the extent that they succeeded in winning the support of the great nobles, and above all of the lesser nobility. The forms in which the policies of the crown developed could easily deceive people about the funda-mentally precarious position of centralised monarchical power, which could be mended neither by reform of the crown's own apparatus of government, nor by the reconstruction of administration and justice, nor again by reform of the militia. As a general rule, however, the limits to the royal power of government were not immediately visible.
It was possible for example for the king to start a war even if Parliament refused its consent. But in the course of this war - as Charles I had to painfully experience - it became more than clear that taxes, soldiers, shipping, horses and provisions could not be obtained in sufficient quantity if those who represented the government of His Majesty in the counties were not willing to make extraordinary efforts in support of the king's policy.
A rationally calculating judgement of royal decrees, however, was blocked both by the traditional notion of obedience and by the newly developing cult of the royal person. Whether a (mainly) practical criticism of royal policy would develop, and on what scale, depended on individual conviction, local structures of the noble hierarchy, and local conditions of ruling practice.
From the 1620s, the possibility for divergence between the policy of the court and local expectations of this policy took the form of an opposition between ‘court' and ‘country'. Court in this connection meant the centre of individual interests.184 This criticism was fuelled by James's inclination towards his favourites - which soon became notorious - and the advancement of new peers by way of patronage. In the 1630s, the criticism of favouritism was sup-plemented by opposition to Charles I's strategy of ‘thorough', i.e. the effort to effectively apply the king's (military) policy in the counties.
We now reach another of the preconditions for crisis that had newly arisen. For whilst, on the one hand, the limitations of royal executive power can hardly be overrated, and must be constantly stressed against new interpreta-tions that see the letter of the law as expressing political reality, it should also be reiterated on the other hand that as the ancien régime developed, the gener-alised royal power literally came to affect people more directly. The relation-ship of monarch to subject, which was previously characterised chiefly by fiscal exploitation and by commands to refrain from particular actions, was now extended to a relationship of regulating intervention - or at least the beginnings of this. This development is most clear in the command of the crown to practise the correct faith in specific politically controlled modalities. To a degree that was previously unknown, however, the reform of the militia (undertaken by Elizabeth) and the more effective handling of social crises (under Charles I) as prescribed in the ‘books of order', represented such an intervention. It was only because the practice of generalised monarchical power was effectively intensified, and estate exercise of this power was de facto checked to a certain extent, that the policies of the crown could be perceived at certain times as a disturbing factor to the implementation of estate interests in the regions.”翻译为中文

宫廷不仅是政策的中心，也是政策的手段。通过为王室人物营造辉煌的环境，宫廷代表了统治的个人结构。在宫廷仪式中，这种统治形式每天都以分配的角色重新上演。随着代表艺术的发展，在艺术史家们过去称之为文艺复兴的历史时期，这种艺术随时间在欧洲各个王室和亲王宫廷中更成功地传播，个人统治的新定义就显得更加令人印象深刻。这不是取代了个人统治在权力和宗教上的基础，而是这种既定的统治结构的表达，以“制度化”的形式普遍化。权力的辉煌不仅本身成为一种权力因素，而且还是其先验和内在的正当性基础。在各个王室和亲王宫廷，个人统治的新的社会呈现形式及其相应的外在属性迅速获得了各种不同的形式。我们不能详细讨论这一发展。以下陈述将限于强调两种情况，这些情况以特定方式影响了英格兰个人君主权力定义的结构性变化。

宫廷的最重要政治功能之一在于世俗化的王室人物崇拜所能施加的说服力。因此，君主周围的仪式给予了国王法令一种特殊的光环，这一点尤其重要，即在英格兰的特定历史环境下，国王人物崇拜的发展具有特殊意义。其起源可追溯到亨利八世的统治时期，即反对教皇统治成为英格兰国王个人统治定义的一部分的时期。起初，这种定义尚未不可改变。直到伊丽莎白统治时期，反教皇主义——即反对被认为是支持教皇统治的观点和政策的斗争——才成为政治主导话语的固定组成部分。这使得对正确信仰的热切追求成为英格兰君主个人权力的新（附加的）基础——在十六世纪末。这可以非常谨慎地描述为个人王权统治中一种民意元素的形成。

伊丽莎白是女性，以及'英格兰'的敌人是天主教徒，这两个事实都有利于这一发展。当伊丽莎白开始统治时，王室已经失去了加来（其在大陆上的最后一个领地），国库空虚，宗教改革在退却，她对王位的要求受到挑战，她被天主教敌人包围。无论加勒特·马丁利描述的伊丽莎白和英格兰人民之间持续了四十五年的“爱之纽带”是否真的存在，或者历史学家们也被好女王贝丝的传说所迷惑，不可否认的是，英格兰王室的实际弱点，以及单身（因而被假定为处女）女性的所谓弱点，被转化为了她个人统治的优势。

这一发展在1585年为保护王室人物免受入侵而成立的“协会”中得到了体现。这一协会的形成回溯到中世纪的封建义务和忠诚的形式，即那些英格兰君主长期以来已经不再声称的义务结构。然而，更重要的是，为民兵组织和建设做出贡献的意愿。集结时伴随的不便之处可以被更容易地忍受，不仅是因为啤酒的前景，而且是因为它被视为为许多人在她在世时就已敬仰为“仙女皇后”的女士服务。
伊丽莎白在世时也有许多关于王室任性的严酷经历，以及关于女王宠臣获得前所未闻的礼物的传闻。然而，不仅是画家、诗人、宫廷人和神职人员理想化了女王。在民间对好女王贝丝的理想化形象中——这种形象在她去世后仍在流传——为个人王权统治的定义增添了一个新元素：这种统治不仅是一个人的财产，而且可以被用作服务于所有人的利益。在女性继承王位和对抗“教皇党”的战争特殊背景下，在十六世纪晚期的英格兰出现了后来在其他地方总结为国家之父或国家之母的统治观念的萌芽。詹姆士一世和查理一世未能满足这一新的王权统治实践期望被认为以特定方式标志着他们的政府的话语（这里提出的解释是，对两位早期斯图亚特君主政府的批评不应仅被理解为对这些政府的具体内容和形式的反应，而是也涉及到在伊丽莎白统治期间出现的新的个人统治观念，从此成为有效的要求）。

在伊丽莎白的精心培养下格外繁荣的“君主的魅力”，在旧制度的所有王室宫廷中都被培养。它们成为“皇室政策”的社会形式发展的组成部分。王室人物的崇拜促进了这一政策的实施，同时王室辉煌和豪华的展示也掩盖了其中的新的危机元素。这些危机在两位斯图亚特国王的统治期间以不同方式变得明显。

王权的个人特性体现在，即使不考虑既定的权力地位和“正当期望”，王室的恩宠也能获得金钱和土地，这种恩宠并不完全可预计，而且继续取决于王室人物的个人倾向和情绪。这种王室恩宠的任意性是宫廷生活社会形式再生产的物质前提，这种生活围绕着一个人的“亲近”组织。同时——对英格兰而言尤其如此——它也是可能引发政府危机的结构性前提条件。这有三个特别原因：因为英格兰的宫廷贵族试图通过赞助在郡和因此在下议院获得并维护政治基础；因为皇冠不能疏远其债权人，尤其是伦敦的特权公司；以及因为新的（阶层）特权可能损害传统的占有形式和统治安全性的维护。

首先看起来可能是次要的肥皂专利案例就表明了特权政策固有的某些困难。大卫·昂德伍德关于下层阶级的文化规范和政治取向的研究明确了这些的潜在范围。当查理一世授权宫廷人物围起林地并将其转变为私有财产，或是抽干沼泽时，首先受到影响的是较贫困的农村人口的再生产前景。然而，如果这些人反抗，他们往往得到了较富裕者甚至当地贵族的支持。此外，这种现代化还影响了较为优越阶层的占有机会，并且最重要的是，它加强了对外来人优惠政策的反对。通过向宫廷人物授予特权，皇冠打击了传统的再生产和统治实践结构，并证明自己对郡的和平与秩序构成威胁。社会冲突因此发展成对政府的批评。反对圈地行为的叛乱并不是表达了阶级之间的对立，而是贪婪的暴发户与捍卫地方秩序者之间的冲突。

新强调王权个人特性不仅意味着这种权力的增长，同时也意味着对它的新风险，并不仅仅在于授予特权的政治要求和同时的政治风险。因为君主和他们的顾问可能本身就被王权的代表所迷惑，并忘记了英格兰国王之所以强大，只是因为他们成功地赢得了大贵族的支持，尤其是小贵族的支持。皇室政策的形式很容易使人误解中央集权王权的根本不稳定地位，这既不能通过改革国王自己的政府机构，也不能通过重建行政和司法机构，更不能通过改革民兵来弥补。然而，通常情况下，王权政府的局限性并不立即显现。

例如，即使议会拒绝同意，国王也可以发动战争。但在这场战争的过程中——正如查理一世不得不痛苦地体验到的——如果那些代表陛下在郡治理的人不愿意为国王的政策做出特殊努力，就无法获得足够数量的税收、士兵、船只、马匹和补给。

然而，对国王法令的理性评判既被传统的服从观念所阻碍，也被新兴的王室人物崇拜所阻碍。是否会发展出对王室政策的（主要是）实践性批评，以及这种批评的规模大小，取决于个人信念、地方贵族等级结构和地方统治实践条件。

从1620年代开始，法庭政策与地方对此政策的期望之间的分歧采取了“朝廷”与“乡村”之间对立的形式。“朝廷”在这里意味着个人利益的中心。这种批评由詹姆士对他的宠臣的倾向——这很快就变得臭名昭著——以及通过赞助晋升新贵族而激发。在1630年代，对宠臣主义的批评被查理一世的“彻底”战略的反对所补充，即努力在郡有效实施国王的（军事）政策。

我们现在达到了新出现的危机前提之一。因为一方面，王权行政权力的局限性几乎不可能被高估，并且必须不断地强调这一点以反对新的解释，这些解释将法律的字面意义视为政治现实的反映，另一方面，也应该重申，随着旧制度的发展，普遍王权实际上直接影响到了人们。君主与臣民之间原本主要以财政剥削和禁止特定行为为特征的关系，现在扩展到了调节干预的关系——或者至少是这种干预的开始。这一发展在英格兰王室命令以特定政治控制的方式实践正确信仰上最为明显。然而，伊丽莎白对民兵的改革（已经实施）和查理一世按照“命令书”规定更有效地处理社会危机，代表了这种干预。只有因为普遍王权的实践实际上得到了加强，并且阶层行使这种权力在一定程度上受到了事实上的限制，皇室的政策才能在某些时候被视为地区实施阶层利益的干扰因素。
